solution.text:
Let us first understand the concept before solving. 

Assume x = 12, y = 5  

Let's assume that we want to divide 12(x) by 5(y) 



Performing above operation by using repeated 
subtraction method then we subtract 5 two times from 
12(x). 

r = x = 12, y =5 

Initially r =12(everything is remaining as we did not 
subtract 5 from 12 any number of times). 

q = 0, because it denotes that how many times 5 has 
been subtracted from 12. 

Now,  
(1) r = 12, y = 5 can we subtract 5 from r? yes, 
because r is greater than y. 

r = 12 - 5 = 7 

q = 0 + 1 = 1(1 signifies that we have subtracted 
5 one time from 12) 
(2) r =7, y = 5 can we subtract 5 from r? yes, 
because r is greater than y. 

r = 7 - 5 = 2 

q = 1 + 1 = 2(2 signifies that we have subtracted 
5 two time from 12) 
(3) r = 2, y = 5 can we subtract 5 from r? no, because 
r is less than y so we need to stop further 
operation. 

 r = 2, q = 2  


Now, solving question by option elimination 
method. 
      Initially q==0 and r = = x 

Before execution of fragment the value of q is 
zero and value of r is x. which matches with 
option c.

Let us take 1st case  

n = 1 \Rightarrow a = 0, b = 0 



This is the maximum element (1), hence loop will not 
run, if there is single element then loop will not run. 

Option (a) 

a!=n 

0!=1(true)  

above condition is true for only one element too. 
Instead, it has to be returned by return statement. 

Hence it is false option. 

Option (b) 

Let us take 2nd case 



1st iteration:  

p[ a] < p[b] \rightarrow true 

a = a + 1 

(Moving a to right side)  



Let us take case 3 



1st iteration: 

p[a] < p[b] \Rightarrow False 

b = b -1 \Rightarrow b = 0 



Case: 4 



(i) 
p[0] < p [4] \Rightarrow false 


b = b -1 \Rightarrow 3 


Why?  


Any Smaller element cannot be maximum if an 
element at ath index is bigger than element at bth 
index then p[b] cannot be maximum \Rightarrow so we 
are moving to the left (skipping this element 
p[b]). 

(ii) p[a] < p [b] \Rightarrow true 


10 < 30 


10 cannot be maximum 


Skip it by moving. 


How  \Rightarrow by a = a + 1 

(iii) p[a] < p [b] \Rightarrow true 


20 < 30 


Skip 20 by a = a + 1  


p[a] < p [b] 


False 


50 < 30 \Rightarrow false  


50 can be maximum, But 30 cannot be maximum. 


b = b =1 


b = 2 






*we are left with one element, this must be a 
maximum element. Now we need not iterate 
further. 


*we need to stop whenever a=b. therefore loop 
condition will be b!=a or a!=b . i.e. option d is 
correct answer.

question.text:
The following program is to be tested for statement 
coverage: 

     begin 
             if (a==b){S1 ; exit; } 

       else if (c==d){S2 ;} 
             else {S3 ; exit; } 

       S4; 

     end 

The test cases T1, T2, T3 and T4 given below are 
expressed in terms of the properties satisfied by the 
values of variables a, b, c and d. The exact values are 
not given. 

T1: a, b, c and d are all equal 

T2: a, b, c and d are all distinct 


T3 : a=b and c !=d 

T4 : a !=b and c=d 

Which of the test suites given below ensures coverage 
of statements S1,  S2,  S3 and S4 ?

solution.text:
T1 : S1 

T1, T2, T4(covers all) 

T2 : S3 

T2, T3, T4(covers all) 

T3 : S1 

T4 : S2, S4 

Hence option (d) is correct answer.

question.text:

are positive integers.

end

(i) 
v & 1: is checking if the rightmost bit of v is 1. 


(If it is 1, then count value is increment by 1). 

(ii) v>> =1: Ensuring that every bit is checked. 


Because count is a static variable, total(v) is 
increasing the value of count by the number of 

set bits in v and also returns the incremented 
value. 

Let us assume that total (5) is called. 

Before total(5)  



5 \Rightarrow 00000101 (only 2 set bits) 

count 


count is increased by 2 and returns the same value. 

Again, if total (4) is called  

Before call 


4 \rightarrow 00000100 

count is increased by 1 and same value is returned  



Now, understanding below code 

for (; i > 0 ; i--){ 

x = x + total (1) 

} 

This loop will execute for i = 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 and  

x = x + total (i) , x is initially 0  

(i)



i = 5  


x = x + total (5) 


5 \Rightarrow 00000101 (2 set bits)  


total (5) \Rightarrow increases the value of count by 2. 


count becomes 2 & same value is returned. 





x = x + total  


0 + 2 


x becomes 2 

(ii) 



i = 4 


x = x + total (4) 


Binary of 4 contains only one set bit, so it will 
increase value of count by 1 and same is 
returned 





x = x + total (4) 


2 + 3  


 x = 5  

(iii) i = 3  





x = x + total (3) 


Binary of 3 contains 2 one's (00000011) that's 
why count is increased by 2 i.e. count becomes 
5 and is reutned. 


x = x + total (3) 


x =  5 + 5 


 x = 10  

(iv) i = 2  





Binary of two contains only one set value so 
count is increased by 1, count becomes 6 and 
same is returned. 


x = x + total (2)  


10 + 6 



 x = 16 

(v)  i = 1 





Binary of 1 contains only one set value so count 
is increased by1, count becomes 7 and same is 
returned,  


x = x+ total (1) 


16 + 7 


 x = 23 


Finally loop terminates and value of x gets 
printed i.e. 23 is printed.

if (b % 2 = = 0) 

{ a = a*a; b= b/2; }// when b is even 

else 

{ 

res = res *a;// executes when b is odd 

b = b -1;} 

Let X = 3, Y = 5 

This code will compute = 35 

Initially, res = 1, a = 3, b = 5 

1st iteration 

As b is odd,  

res = 1*3 = 3 

b = 5 - 1 = 4 

After 1st iteration 

a = 3, b = 4, res = 3, X = 3, Y = 5 

Let us check all option after 

1st iteration (before 2nd iteration) 

(a) XY = ab 


35 = 34(false) 

(b) (res*a)Y = (res*X)b 

(3*3)5 = (3*3)4(false) 

(c) XY = res*ab 

35 = 3*34(true) 
        (d)  XY = (res*a)b 

35 = (3*3)4(false) 

Hence option C is correct option (this is option 
elimination method).

question.text:
Consider the following pseudo code. What is the total 
number of multiplications to be performed? 

D = 2 

for i = 1 to n do 

   for j = i to n do 

       for k = j + 1  to n do        

         D=D * 3

options.text: Half of the product of the 3 consecutive integers.

One-third of the product of the 3 consecutive 
integers.

One-sixth of the product of the 3 consecutive 
integers.
